edits.slides.0.description: In my vibe coding course, students shipped apps that broke on every device but their own. I built a tool to fix that. Lesson: remove the switching cost and people use it.
edits.slides.1.description: Apps shipped fine on the student's screen — broken everywhere else. Four reasons.
edits.slides.1.issues.0.description: Built and tested on their own monitor — never checked other viewports until something broke.
edits.slides.1.issues.1.description: DevTools device mode is powerful but intimidating — the mental overhead made students avoid it.
edits.slides.1.issues.2.description: Switching between editor and browser broke the coding flow. Most students just skipped the check.
edits.slides.1.issues.3.title: No real device frame
edits.slides.1.issues.3.description: A pixel-width number doesn't feel like a phone. Without a real frame, the size didn't stick.
edits.slides.2.content: I observed it — consistent enough to act on.
edits.slides.2.issues.0: Projects shipped with broken mobile layouts
edits.slides.2.issues.1: DevTools usage stayed low — even among students who knew it
edits.slides.3.content: The barrier wasn't knowledge — it was friction. Every responsive check meant leaving the editor, opening a browser, navigating DevTools. That cost was enough to skip it. Friction problems in SaaS look exactly the same.
edits.slides.3.issues.0: Stay inside the editor — no switching tabs
edits.slides.3.issues.2: One click to any device, no setup
edits.slides.7.description: Old workflow: five steps to a number. New: one click to a frame.
edits.slides.7.beforeDescription: Left the editor, opened DevTools, typed a viewport width, guessed at a device size — then switched back to code.
edits.slides.7.afterDescription: One click to the panel, pick a device, see a real frame — still inside the editor.
edits.slides.10.outcomes.0.title: Testing became habitual
edits.slides.12.whatWorked.0: Placement was the strategy — it was already in the editor.
edits.slides.12.whatWorked.1: CSS frames made the size feel real. Students took the checks more seriously.
edits.slides.12.whatYoudDoDifferently.0: I'd have built side-by-side from day one. Students kept asking for both views.
edits.slides.12.whatYouLearned: The goal wasn't completeness — it was changing a habit. Friction is a design decision.
edits.slides.12.whatYouCouldntMeasure: Cleaner demos were visible. Long-term habit change is harder to trace.
